Title: Viral Small Interfering RNAs Target Host Genes to Mediate Disease Symptoms in Plants

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter (constitutive); tobacco rubisco small subunit promoter/SSU-P (green tissue-associated)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photosynthetic light harvesting and plant green pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Magnesium chelation step of chlorophyll branch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in green tissues/leaves; transcript assayed in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf tissues; reduced strongly upon CMV Y-Sat infection in chlorotic leaves
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: CMV Y-satellite infection; CMV helper virus alone did not reduce CHLI expression; plants grown at 25°C glasshouse with natural light
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the ATP-dependent magnesium chelatase step inserting Mg2+ into protoporphyrin IX to initiate the chlorophyll branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly required for synthesis of chlorophyll end products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control/essential step for chlorophyll biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing of CHLI dramatically reduces chlorophyll accumulation and causes yellowing/chlorosis; silencing-resistant CHLI prevents Y-Sat-induced yellowing symptoms
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHLI contains a 22-nt sequence complementary to the CMV Y-Sat yellow domain. 2. CHLI mRNA is dramatically down-regulated in Y-Sat-infected tobacco, correlating with chlorosis severity and Y-Sat siRNA accumulation. 3. 5′ RACE confirmed siRNA-directed cleavage of CHLI at the predicted target region. 4. CHLI-RNAi transgenic tobacco phenocopied Y-Sat-induced chlorosis. 5. A silencing-resistant CHLI variant with silent mutations fully abolished Y-Sat yellowing symptoms while maintaining CHLI transcript accumulation. 6. Natural SNPs in the targeted CHLI region in resistant Nicotiana species prevented CHLI silencing and symptom development.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Target prediction/BLAST; CHLI-RNAi transgenics; northern blot of CHLI mRNA; chlorophyll measurement; 5′ RACE cleavage mapping; small RNA deep sequencing; transgenic complementation with wild-type and silencing-resistant CHLI; comparative sequencing across Nicotiana species
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Small RNA deep sequencing (Solexa); approximately 4 million small RNA reads reported; accession not provided
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong CHLI silencing caused severe chlorosis to complete bleaching; overexpression of wild-type CHLI delayed but did not prevent symptom onset; engineered resistance may be challenged by viral sequence variation/quasispecies
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High value as a target-protection strategy: introducing multiple silent mutations into the viral siRNA target site of CHLI can prevent symptom-causing host gene silencing without altering protein sequence; useful for engineering resistance to viral siRNA-mediated disease, though sequence-diverse viral variants remain a risk
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The causal mechanism is not altered enzyme activity per se but viral siRNA-mediated post-transcriptional silencing of CHLI. Disease-resistant Nicotiana species (N. clevelandii, N. debneyi) carry natural sequence variation in the targeted CHLI region, explaining species specificity of symptom development. The paper also tests mutant transgene forms (mtCHLI/gmtCHLI) as silencing-resistant variants.
